stories.body: Venezuela’s twin earthquakes this week have killed at least 1,430 people, injured 3,200 and left 3,100 homeless, while nearly 68,900 remain unaccounted for, according to National Assembly president Jorge Rodríguez, as improvised civilian rescue crews and more than 14,000 security personnel dig through flattened buildings in hard-hit coastal areas such as La Guaira and Caraballeda, where an 11-year-old boy was pulled alive from rubble; the United Nations now estimates $6.7 billion in damage—about 6% of national GDP—and foreign teams face delays entering the country because Simón Bolívar International Airport was damaged, stranding a British Serve On contingent in Madrid for more than a day.
stories.headline: Venezuela earthquakes: death toll pushes past 1,400 as UN warns millions affected

stories.body: The United States bombed Iranian targets in Sirik, Bandar-e Lengeh and Qeshm Island for a second straight day after a drone attack on a commercial vessel near the Strait of Hormuz, prompting Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps to fire missiles and drones at U.S. bases in Bahrain and Kuwait, where air-raid sirens and air defenses were activated; as the regional confrontation enters its 121st day, Israel also struck southern Lebanon despite a tentative framework to halt fighting there.
stories.headline: US-Iran tit-for-tat escalates with strikes on Bahrain and Kuwait

stories.body: Iran has insisted that any interim U.S.-Iran peace deal must include a ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah and an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon, with Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi and adviser Mohsen Rezaei reiterating Tehran’s commitment to its Lebanese ally and warning Israel against continued operations, while Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem rejected a U.S.-brokered framework he called humiliating and vowed to keep fighting as Israeli strikes killed at least one person in southern Lebanon and protesters in Beirut denounced the agreement.
stories.headline: Tehran ties any peace deal to Hezbollah ceasefire as it backs Lebanese ally

stories.body: Iran is studying a proposed interim deal with the United States to halt their war but is taking what Mehr News described as a stern approach after months of mistrust, leaving the Strait of Hormuz largely closed and global oil markets volatile as Israel continues strikes in southern Lebanon and Iran seeks concessions including sanctions relief, access to oil revenues, and leverage over key maritime chokepoints while threatening to expand its blockade to the Bab el-Mandeb if Beirut is hit again.
stories.headline: Iran reviews proposed truce while Strait of Hormuz remains largely shut

stories.body: NASA ordered five astronauts to shelter in their docked Crew Dragon for about two hours after an air leak aboard Russia’s Zvezda service module doubled from one to two pounds of air per day, but reversed the evacuation order once Roscosmos paused a plan to saw into the module to reach the crack, with both agencies pledging a collaborative fix as the 27-year-old International Space Station—never yet fully evacuated—continues housing seven crew members amid ongoing U.S. legislative efforts to extend its life to 2032.
stories.headline: NASA aborts ISS evacuation after reassessing Russian module air leak

stories.body: Kyiv faced intense Russian missile strikes early Tuesday, sending residents into shelters as fires broke out in the Podil district and a nine-story apartment building burned after debris hit its roof, while cars ignited in Obolon and open areas near a kindergarten were ablaze; Mayor Vitali Klitschko and President Volodymyr Zelenskiy warned residents to heed air raid alerts amid Russian threats of systematic attacks on the capital’s military and decision-making centers following deadly strikes in occupied Luhansk.
stories.headline: Kyiv endures heavy Russian missile barrage as apartment blocks burn

stories.body: Senegal’s Pastef party, led by recently ousted Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko, will stay out of President Bassirou Diomaye Faye’s next government following disagreements over the party’s role, even as Pastef holds a parliamentary majority and Sonko was reinstated as an MP and elected speaker; the split comes as Senegal seeks to resolve a debt crisis triggered by previously hidden liabilities that pushed debt to 132% of GDP, leading the IMF to freeze its $1.8 billion program until talks resume later this month.
stories.headline: Senegal’s Pastef party refuses to join new government amid debt crisis

stories.body: The U.S. Senate voted 52-47 along party lines to send a $70 billion immigration enforcement package to the House, boosting funding for ICE and Border Patrol while refusing to permanently block Donald Trump’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund despite bipartisan attempts to reallocate or eliminate it; Republicans cited assurances from acting Attorney General nominee Todd Blanche that the fund will not proceed, but Democrats warn the money could still compensate Trump allies as the legislation funds deportation crackdowns for the next three years.
stories.headline: US Senate approves $70 billion immigration enforcement bill while Trump fund fight rages

stories.body: The U.S. Supreme Court, in a 6-3 decision, lifted a lower-court block on the government’s border “metering” policy, potentially allowing the Trump administration to again cap daily asylum applications at ports of entry—an approach first used under Obama and expanded by Trump that forced thousands of migrants to wait in dangerous encampments—though the Department of Homeland Security has not said whether it will reimplement the limits, calling the ruling an important tool for securing the southern border despite legal arguments that asylum seekers must be processed upon arrival.
stories.headline: Supreme Court greenlights revival of restrictive asylum policy

stories.body: About 3 million people—13% of enrollees—dropped Affordable Care Act coverage between February 2025 and February 2026, shrinking participation to 19.2 million as federal data and health analysts point to the January expiration of enhanced subsidies that triggered steep premium hikes, with KFF estimating enrollment could slide to 17.5 million this year despite government suggestions that anti-fraud efforts played a role, underscoring voter concern over healthcare affordability ahead of November elections.
stories.headline: Millions leave Obamacare plans after enhanced subsidies lapse

stories.body: Representative Julia Letlow secured the Republican nomination for Louisiana’s U.S. Senate seat by defeating state Treasurer John Fleming in a runoff shaped by dueling claims of MAGA loyalty after incumbent Bill Cassidy was ousted in the first round; buoyed by endorsements from Donald Trump, Governor Jeff Landry and House Majority Leader Steve Scalise, Letlow is now the overwhelming favorite to win in deep-red Louisiana and deliver another Trump-aligned senator while critics highlight her past support for diversity initiatives that she has since renounced.
stories.headline: Trump-backed Julia Letlow wins Louisiana GOP Senate runoff, cementing MAGA grip

stories.body: Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez told the PSOE Federal Committee he understands grassroots anger over corruption probes involving senior figures, his wife Begoña Gómez and brother David, but vowed to govern through 2027, insisting there is no illegal party financing, defending José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero’s legacy and promising to “clean what must be cleaned” while contrasting the Socialists’ swift expulsions with what he called the PP’s connivance, even as he acknowledged recent scandals such as those involving Santos Cerdán and José Luis Ábalos.
stories.headline: Sánchez rallies PSOE to stay the course through 2027 despite corruption anger

stories.body: Castilla-La Mancha president Emiliano García-Page again broke ranks at the PSOE Federal Committee, urging Pedro Sánchez to call early elections or seek a confidence vote, arguing the party is in its worst recent moment after corruption revelations and warning that the PSOE must put its 150-year legacy above any leader’s survival, with Page accusing the national leadership of ignoring successive regional defeats and insisting that if everything has been handled well there should be no fear in letting voters decide.
stories.headline: Castilla-La Mancha’s Page demands elections or confidence vote, warning PSOE is bigger than any leader

stories.body: People’s Party leader Alberto Núñez Feijóo, campaigning in Barcelona alongside Catalan PP chief Alejandro Fernández, pressed Junts per Catalunya to act “responsibly” by supporting steps that would force early national elections—potentially via a confidence vote or other parliamentary maneuvers—arguing Spain “is playing for democracy” amid corruption cases encircling Pedro Sánchez, while setting a goal of winning 12 Catalan seats in Congress and promising not to treat Catalonia as a bargaining chip if the PP returns to power.
stories.headline: Feijóo urges Junts to be “responsible” and trigger national elections

stories.body: Thousands marched through central Barcelona on the eve of International LGBTI+ Liberation Day in the 50th consecutive protest since 1977, demanding social and labor rights that do not hinge on marriage and rejecting the commercial Pride Barcelona parade as an “enterprise party,” with activists such as Clara Morató-Aragonès of the Catalan Asexual Association warning that queer people still face aggression and exclusion while calling for recognition of diverse relationship models beyond monogamous unions and linking rising attacks to the far right’s growth.
stories.headline: Barcelona Pride march demands LGTBI+ rights beyond marriage and rejects corporate parade

stories.body: Barcelona’s Venezuelan diaspora, coordinated through the SOS Venezuela platform of about 15 entities, is prioritizing cash donations and logistics planning before launching material collections to support earthquake-stricken regions back home, contacting the local Venezuelan consulate, city hall and Generalitat to secure storage and transport while noting immediate needs such as body bags, as the city pledges €300,000 in emergency subsidies and Catalan emergency teams deploy to Venezuela with volunteers stressing that aid will be required for months.
stories.headline: Barcelona’s Venezuelan community mobilises aid convoys after twin quakes

stories.body: Neighbourhood platform Maragall Respira and allied associations relaunched their long-standing demand for a protected bike lane along Passeig de Maragall—connecting Carrer del Tajo to Avinguda de l’Estatut—criticizing city spending on hosting the Tour de France Grand Départ while the promised lane remains only on paper; activists unveiled “The Tour passes, the lane stays” shirts, insist their campaign targets safer local mobility rather than the race itself, and argue cycling infrastructure should be the lasting legacy of the Tour’s visit to Barcelona.
stories.headline: Passeig de Maragall neighbours step up push for protected bike lane ahead of Tour de France
